**Task Documentation for Student Data Analysts**  
This document will include information student data analysts can use to familiarize themselves with tools like R, GitHub, and .csv files. After reading through this documentation, you will be able to open code in R, publish a website on Github, and edit a .csv file from My Favorite Albums. 

*How to open My Favorite Albums code in R* 

- Prerequisites: R and RStudio must already be installed. You must also have RStudio running.   
1. Navigate to this Github page: [https://github.com/UW-Example-Student/MyFavoriteAlbums](https://github.com/UW-Example-Student/MyFavoriteAlbums) 

You are in the right place if you see these options: 

![][image1]

2. Click the **albums\_by\_band.R** file at the top of the page. You will be taken to a preview of the code.  
3. In the top right corner of the preview page, click the button circled in the screenshot below to download the raw file.   
   
    ![][image2]  
4. Click the downloaded **albums\_by\_band.R** file. It automatically opens in RStudio.

You will be directed to the code used in My Favorite Albums that corresponds to the **Top Albums by Year** page on this website: [https://cholstro.shinyapps.io/shiny-music/](https://cholstro.shinyapps.io/shiny-music/) 

![][image3]   
\*Note: This is just an example of how to open code for one specific part of the My Favorite Albums website. To make the My Favorite Albums app work, you will need to download all files. Repeat these steps for any file in the GitHub page mentioned in step 1 to open the rest of the code in RStudio. 

*Uploading code to a repository using GitHub*

- Prerequisites: You must already have created a GitHub account and have a file that you would like to upload to GitHub. For the purposes of these instructions, the code from the previously described ‘How to open My Favorite Albums code in R’ will be used. You can upload any code in place of the one used in this example, just make sure you have it saved in a place where you can access it easily.   
1. Navigate to your GitHub repository by hovering over your profile picture and clicking **Repositories**.  
     
     
     
     
     
     
   

2. On the top right of the page, click the green **New** button accompanied by a book icon. Then a new page called **Create a new repository** opens.  
3. Under **General**, enter the name for your new repository. Any name may be used but it is suggested that to use a concise, consistent, and descriptive name.  
   1. For example, a descriptive name for the code used in the previous task would be ‘Albums by Band Code’.  
4. Under **Description**, enter a brief summary of the project’s function and purpose. Add any additional information here you or someone else may want to reference.  
   1. For example, the **Albums by Band Code** might have a description like: ‘Code for data and albums used in ‘My Favorite Albums’ software’. However, because we are planning on uploading all files needed for My Favorite Albums, a name like ‘Code for My Favorite Albums’ is more appropriate.   
5. Under **Configuration**, choose **public** visibility to allow others to see this new repository.  
6. Keep **Add Read** selected to ‘off’ unless wanting to add a longer description and overview of the project.  
7. Keep **No .gitignore** selected because we are not interested in telling GitHub which files not to track.  
8. Keep **No license** selected because the code is under exclusive copyright by default. \*Note: Steps 3-8 may be adjusted based on goals and demands of the project.   
9. Click the green **Create repository** button after checking the interface looks like this: 

![][image4]

You will be taken to a page that provides a https link for your repository. Make note of this URL.

10. Under the URL, click the blue hyperlink called **uploading an existing file**.  
    1. Doing so will take you to a page to **choose your files** from your computer. Here you should select the Albums by Band code, or any other code you want to publish, as referenced in the prerequisites section of this task.  
11. Click the green **Commit changes** button at the bottom of the page. Doing so will upload the code to your repository.

\*Note: This is just an example of how to upload code for one specific part of the My Favorite Albums website to the repository. To make the My Favorite Albums app work, you will need to upload all files from My Favorite Albums to the GitHub repository. 

12. Now that the repository is created, add the rest of the necessary files from My Favorite Albums. Navigate to your repository.  
13. Click **Add file** button to the left of the green **Code** button.   
14. Click **Upload files** from the dropdown menu.  
15. Select the blue **choose your files** button and select the rest of the My Favorite Albums files needed. 

\*Note: It is helpful to have all files downloaded into one folder for easy selection. 

*Updating a .csv file with Your Data*

1. Begin by navigating to this Github page: [https://github.com/UW-Example-Student/MyFavoriteAlbums](https://github.com/UW-Example-Student/MyFavoriteAlbums)   
2. In the repository, click the blue **data** folder at the top. A folder opens. There should be only one file within the folder: **album-rankings.csv**.  
3. Click **album-rankings.csv**.  
   1. As shown below, a preview of the .csv file used for the album ranking software appears.  
4. At the top right corner of the page, click the icon with an arrow pointing down.

![][image5]

Doing so will download the raw data.

5. Click on the newly downloaded .csv file.  
6. Each row of data represents a year, ranking, artist, rating, and information if the music is available on vinyl, if it’s an EP/live album. To edit each row for your own favorite albums, double click each cell and input information about a favorite album of your choice. 

For example, I have added three albums of my own: ![][image6]

7. Repeat down the list of the original .csv file by adding new albums of your own\!  
8. Save to your computer.
